summary: I grew up in Santiago, Chile, where I spent my childhood before moving to the United States in 2011 with my family. Santiago was the city where my early memories were formed, but my heart often drifts to the south of Chile where my grandparents lived. Although I lived in Santiago, the traditions from the south, like drinking mate, were always a part of my life.

Chile is my home country where I was born and raised, specifically in Santiago. Every year during my childhood, I traveled to the south of Chile to visit my grandparents and relatives, especially in places like Pucón. Drinking mate is a cherished tradition in Chile, particularly in the southern regions, and it connects me deeply to my roots and family history.

In 2011, my whole family and I moved to the United States, which was a big change from living in Chile. When I arrived here, my father gave me a new mate cup, which made me feel connected to my childhood and traditions despite being far from home. Living in the United States has made me appreciate the cultural practices from Chile even more.

Yerba mate, or simply mate as we call it, was a big part of my life growing up in Chile. I used to drink mate that belonged to others, but when I moved to the United States, my father gave me my own mate cup. Drinking mate reminds me of my childhood visits to my grandparents in the south of Chile, where mate was shared daily and was a symbol of family and conversation.

Pucón is a local town in the south of Chile where my grandparents used to live, and it's a place full of memories for me. In Pucón, the tradition of drinking mate is very communal, with one person preparing and serving the mate cup to a group, sometimes up to ten people. This tradition reflects the close-knit family gatherings I experienced during my childhood visits there.
